a.xlink:title: Specifies how the model is optimized and scored, including loss design, optimizer state, precision, and benchmark harness constraints. These choices strongly affect peak memory and convergence at small scale.
Defines the training objective that shapes gradients. Loss modifications can change calibration and stability without adding parameters to the model.
Controls parameter updates and determines additional optimizer-state memory. Optimizer choice is a major contributor to peak training memory at fixed parameter counts.
Chooses numeric formats for weights, activations, and gradients. Lower precision reduces memory and bandwidth but can require stabilization tricks to train reliably.
Controls how intermediate activations are stored for backpropagation. Checkpointing can reduce peak memory by recomputing activations at the cost of extra compute.
Defines how the model is scored and constrained, including exact benchmark implementation. A stable harness is required to compare innovations under the same Parameter Golf rules.
Tracks parameter count and memory usage to enforce constraints and guide component swaps. Accurate accounting enables deliberate tradeoffs rather than accidental OOM failures.
Maximizes the likelihood of the next token under the model distribution. It is the standard objective for language modeling and typically parameter-free.
Blends the one-hot target with a uniform distribution to reduce overconfidence. This can improve generalization and calibration in small models.
Adds a penalty to stabilize logits and improve numerical behavior, often helpful with low precision. It can improve training stability without changing model size.
Represents the extra state stored per parameter (e.g., moments) and its precision. Under tight memory budgets, quantized or factorized states can free memory for larger batches or models.
Stores first and second moment estimates for each parameter, typically in FP32 for stability. This is effective but can dominate training memory for tiny models.
Quantizes optimizer moments to reduce memory while approximating Adam updates. This can significantly lower peak memory with modest accuracy impact when tuned.
Approximates second-moment statistics with factored representations to reduce memory. It is often used when optimizer-state memory is the main bottleneck.
Uses momentum buffers but avoids second-moment estimates, reducing state memory. It can be harder to tune for transformers but is memory-light.
Uses 32-bit floats for most tensors, offering stability at high memory cost. This often violates Parameter Golf memory constraints for longer contexts.
Uses half precision for activations/weights with selective FP32 accumulations. It is a standard approach to fit larger models/batches under memory limits.
Uses 8-bit floats for some tensors to further reduce memory and bandwidth. It can provide large savings but often needs careful scaling and kernel support.
Keeps forward activations for backpropagation, minimizing recomputation. This is compute-efficient but often hits memory ceilings first.
Recomputes some forward passes during backpropagation instead of storing all activations. This reduces peak memory and can unlock larger batch sizes in Parameter Golf.
Scores models under strict parameter and memory constraints, preventing trivial scaling. The benchmark incentivizes architectural and optimization innovations that fit tight budgets.
Computes model parameter count, optionally by component, to ensure compliance with size limits. It enables targeted parameter reallocation toward novel modules.
Estimates peak memory from activations, optimizer state, and KV cache to prevent exceeding constraints. It supports 'swap out' reasoning for memory-heavy components like attention.
